annotations.label: Cardinal number

anchor.snippet: a cardinal number , or cardinal for short, is a kind of number that measures the cardinality of a set

annotations.note: `Cardinal` is the quotient of types by equinumerosity, with `Cardinal.mk α` (#α) the cardinality of a type.

annotations.label: Same cardinality (bijection)

anchor.snippet: there is a one-to-one correspondence (bijection) between the elements of the two sets

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq : #α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` states equal cardinality means a bijection exists.

annotations.label: Naturals and rationals equinumerous

anchor.snippet: there exists a bijection between the set of all natural numbers

annotations.note: `Denumerable ℚ` provides a bijection ℚ ≃ ℕ, giving #ℚ = ℵ₀ = #ℕ via `Cardinal.mk_denumerable`.

annotations.label: Cantor: infinite sets can differ in cardinality

anchor.snippet: a fundamental theorem due to Georg Cantor shows that it is possible for two infinite sets to have different cardinalities

annotations.note: `Cardinal.cantor : a < 2 ^ a` applied to an infinite `a` produces two infinite sets of distinct cardinality.

annotations.label: Aleph-null as smallest aleph

anchor.snippet: since it is the smallest aleph number

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph_zero : ℵ_ 0 = ℵ₀` together with `aleph0_le_aleph` makes ℵ₀ the least aleph.

annotations.label: Sameness of cardinality is an equivalence relation

anchor.snippet: Sameness of cardinality is an equivalence relation

annotations.note: `Cardinal.isEquivalent : Setoid (Type u)` is exactly equinumerosity proven reflexive, symmetric, transitive.

annotations.label: Equivalence class corresponds to a cardinal

anchor.snippet: Every equivalence class of sets under equinumerosity corresponds to a cardinal number

annotations.note: `Cardinal` is literally the quotient by `Cardinal.isEquivalent` and `mk` sends a type to its class.

annotations.label: Hilbert's Grand Hotel bijection

anchor.snippet: A classic example is Hilbert's paradox of the Grand Hotel

annotations.note: The underlying identity `add_one_eq : ℵ₀ ≤ a → a + 1 = a` is formalized, but the Grand Hotel scenario itself is not named.

annotations.label: Plus-one need not give a new cardinal

anchor.snippet: does not always construct a new cardinal number

annotations.note: `Cardinal.add_one_eq : ℵ₀ ≤ a → a + 1 = a` shows adding one to an infinite cardinal yields the same cardinal.

annotations.label: Power set gives strictly greater cardinality

anchor.snippet: the power set operation always results in a strictly greater cardinality

annotations.note: `Cardinal.cantor : a < 2 ^ a`, with `mk_powerset`/`mk_set` giving #(𝒫 s) = 2 ^ #s.

annotations.label: More reals than naturals

anchor.snippet: there are strictly more real numbers than natural numbers

annotations.note: `mk_real : #ℝ = 𝔠` with `aleph0_lt_continuum : ℵ₀ < 𝔠` gives strictly more reals than naturals.

annotations.label: Cardinality function

anchor.snippet: The cardinality function is a cardinal function that takes in a set

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk : Type u → Cardinal` is the cardinality function (notation #).

anchors.snippet: The cardinality function is a cardinal function that takes in a set

annotations.label: Axiom of cardinality / Hume's principle

anchor.snippet: is sometimes called the axiom of cardinality

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq : #α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` is exactly Hume's principle for the mk function.

anchors.snippet: is sometimes called the axiom of cardinality

annotations.label: Weak cardinal assignment (Moschovakis)

anchor.snippet: Moschovakis defines a (weak) cardinal assignment as an operation

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk` satisfies the weak-assignment properties (`mk_congr`, `outMkEquiv`), but Moschovakis's abstract notion is not defined.

annotations.label: Strong cardinal assignment

anchor.snippet: then it is a strong cardinal assignment

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk` is a strong cardinal assignment (`Cardinal.eq` gives the iff), but the general meta-notion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Von Neumann cardinal assignment

anchor.snippet: the von Neumann cardinal assignment , which represents the cardinality of a set

annotations.note: `Cardinal.ord` maps a cardinal to its least (initial) ordinal, but Mathlib's cardinals are quotients, not von Neumann cardinals.

annotations.label: Initial ordinal

anchor.snippet: is also known as the initial ordinal of the cardinal number

annotations.note: `Ordinal.IsInitial o ↔ o.card.ord = o` defines an initial ordinal as the least of its cardinality.

annotations.label: All finite ordinals are initial ordinals

anchor.snippet: all finite ordinals have different cardinalities, and thus all finite ordinals are initial ordinals

annotations.note: `Ordinal.isInitial_natCast (n : ℕ) : IsInitial n` states every finite ordinal is initial.

annotations.label: Many infinite ordinals share a cardinality

anchor.snippet: many different infinite ordinal numbers can have the same cardinality

annotations.note: `isInitial_succ : IsInitial (succ o) ↔ o < ω` implies successors of infinite ordinals share their cardinality, but no single decl asserts the general fact.

annotations.label: Aleph number notation

anchor.snippet: infinite initial ordinal is written

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph : Ordinal ↪o Cardinal` with notation `ℵ_ o` indexes the infinite cardinals.

annotations.label: Smallest uncountable ordinal

anchor.snippet: is the smallest uncountable ordinal

annotations.note: `ω₁ := Ordinal.omega 1` is the first uncountable ordinal, with `omega0_lt_omega_one`.

annotations.label: Infinite initial ordinals are limit ordinals

anchor.snippet: Infinite initial ordinals are limit ordinals

annotations.note: `isSuccLimit_omega (o) : IsSuccLimit (ω_ o)`, and the infinite initial ordinals are exactly the `ω_ o`.

annotations.label: Cardinality as class of equinumerous sets

anchor.snippet: is as the class [ X ] of all sets that are equinumerous with X

annotations.note: Mathlib realizes the equinumerosity class via a quotient (small type) rather than a class of all equinumerous sets.

annotations.label: [X] is a proper class

anchor.snippet: by the axiom of limitation of size , [ X ] is a proper class

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Cardinal.{u}` is a (small) quotient type, so equinumerosity classes are never treated as proper classes.

annotations.label: Scott's trick (least rank)

anchor.snippet: if we restrict from this class to those equinumerous with X that have the least rank

annotations.note: Mathlib defines cardinals as quotient types and does not use Scott's least-rank trick.

annotations.label: Lévy mixed cardinal definition

anchor.snippet: as the von Neumann cardinal when

annotations.note: No mixed von-Neumann/Scott cardinal definition appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order on cardinals

anchor.snippet: means that there exists an injective function from X to Y

annotations.note: `Cardinal.le_def : #α ≤ #β ↔ Nonempty (α ↪ β)` defines the order via injections.

annotations.label: Cantor–Bernstein–Schroeder theorem

anchor.snippet: The Cantor–Bernstein–Schroeder theorem states that

annotations.note: `schroeder_bernstein` (and `Embedding.antisymm`) gives a bijection from mutual injections; this is `le_antisymm` for cardinals.

annotations.label: AC equivalent to cardinal comparability

anchor.snippet: The axiom of choice is equivalent to the statement that given two sets X and Y

annotations.note: Comparability (`Embedding.total`, `Cardinal.linearOrder`) is proved using Lean's built-in choice, but its equivalence to AC is not formalized.

annotations.label: Dedekind-infinite / Dedekind-finite

anchor.snippet: A set X is called Dedekind-infinite if there exists a proper subset Y of X

annotations.note: Mathlib has the `Infinite`/`Finite` typeclasses but no Dedekind-infinite definition for sets via proper-subset bijection.

annotations.label: Finite cardinals are the natural numbers

anchor.snippet: The finite cardinals are just the natural numbers

annotations.note: `lt_aleph0 : c < ℵ₀ ↔ ∃ n : ℕ, c = n` and `range_natCast` identify finite cardinals with ℕ.

annotations.label: Dedekind-infinite implies infinite

anchor.snippet: any Dedekind-infinite set is infinite

annotations.note: There is no Dedekind-infinite notion in Mathlib, so this implication is not stated.

annotations.label: Dedekind notions match standard under AC

anchor.snippet: the Dedekind notions correspond to the standard ones

annotations.note: Without a Dedekind-finiteness definition, this equivalence under choice is not formalized.

annotations.label: Aleph numbers

anchor.snippet: The aleph numbers are the cardinalities of well-orderable infinite sets

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph` enumerates the infinite cardinals indexed by ordinals.

anchors.snippet: The aleph numbers are the cardinalities of well-orderable infinite sets

annotations.label: An aleph for every ordinal

anchor.snippet: there exists an aleph number

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph : Ordinal ↪o Cardinal` is a total order embedding, giving an aleph for each ordinal.

annotations.label: Under AC all infinite cardinals are alephs

anchor.snippet: all infinite cardinal numbers are aleph numbers

annotations.note: `mem_range_aleph_iff : c ∈ range aleph ↔ ℵ₀ ≤ c` (also `range_aleph = Ici ℵ₀`) holds since Lean assumes choice.

anchors.snippet: all infinite cardinal numbers are aleph numbers

annotations.label: Non-aleph cardinals incomparable (Hartogs)

anchor.snippet: must be incomparable to some aleph number by Hartogs's theorem

annotations.note: Hartogs's theorem is absent; with Lean's choice all cardinals are alephs and comparable, so this choice-free scenario is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finite cardinal arithmetic agrees with naturals

anchor.snippet: these operations coincide with the usual operations for natural numbers

annotations.note: The cast ℕ → Cardinal is a semiring hom (Nat.cast_add/mul) with `power_natCast`, so finite arithmetic matches ℕ.

annotations.label: Successor cardinal

anchor.snippet: every cardinal κ has a successor, denoted κ +

annotations.note: Cardinals carry a `SuccOrder` instance, so `Order.succ c` is the least cardinal greater than `c`.

annotations.label: Minimal successor cardinal without AC (Hartogs)

anchor.snippet: using Hartogs' theorem , it can be shown that for any cardinal number κ , there is a minimal cardinal

annotations.note: The minimal larger cardinal exists via the `SuccOrder Cardinal` instance, but the Hartogs-based choice-free construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cardinal addition via disjoint union

anchor.snippet: addition is given by the union of X and Y

annotations.note: `add_def : #α + #β = #(α ⊕ β)` defines addition via the disjoint union.

annotations.label: Zero is additive identity

anchor.snippet: Zero is an additive identity

annotations.note: The `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance proves `zero_add`/`add_zero`.

annotations.label: Addition is associative

anchor.snippet: Addition is associative

annotations.note: `add_assoc` is provided by the `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance.

annotations.label: Addition is commutative

anchor.snippet: Addition is commutative

annotations.note: `add_comm` is provided by the `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance.

annotations.label: Addition is non-decreasing

anchor.snippet: Addition is non-decreasing in both arguments

annotations.note: `addLeftMono`/`addRightMono` instances give monotonicity of addition in both arguments.

annotations.label: Infinite cardinal addition under AC

anchor.snippet: addition of infinite cardinal numbers is easy

annotations.note: `add_eq_max : ℵ₀ ≤ a → a + b = max a b` computes infinite cardinal sums.

annotations.label: Cardinal subtraction

anchor.snippet: there exists a cardinal κ such that μ + κ = σ if and only if μ ≤ σ

annotations.note: The `CanonicallyOrderedAdd Cardinal` instance yields `le_iff_exists_add`: μ ≤ σ ↔ ∃ κ, σ = μ + κ.

annotations.label: Cardinal multiplication via Cartesian product

anchor.snippet: The product of cardinals comes from the Cartesian product

annotations.note: `mul_def : #α * #β = #(α × β)` defines multiplication via the Cartesian product.

annotations.label: Zero is multiplicative absorbing element

anchor.snippet: Zero is a multiplicative absorbing element

annotations.note: `zero_mul`/`mul_zero` come from the `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance.

annotations.label: No nontrivial zero divisors

anchor.snippet: There are no nontrivial zero divisors

annotations.note: `Cardinal.noZeroDivisors` instance proves a * b = 0 → a = 0 ∨ b = 0.

annotations.label: One is multiplicative identity

anchor.snippet: One is a multiplicative identity

annotations.note: `one_mul`/`mul_one` come from the `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance.

annotations.label: Multiplication is associative

anchor.snippet: Multiplication is associative

annotations.note: `mul_assoc` is provided by the `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance.

annotations.label: Multiplication is commutative

anchor.snippet: Multiplication is commutative

annotations.note: `mul_comm` is provided by the `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance.

annotations.label: Multiplication is non-decreasing

anchor.snippet: Multiplication is non-decreasing in both arguments

annotations.note: The ordered-semiring structure (`mul_le_mul'`) gives monotonicity of multiplication.

annotations.label: Multiplication distributes over addition

anchor.snippet: Multiplication distributes over addition

annotations.note: `left_distrib`/`right_distrib` come from the `CommSemiring Cardinal` instance.

annotations.label: Infinite cardinal multiplication under AC

anchor.snippet: multiplication of infinite cardinal numbers is also easy

annotations.note: `mul_eq_max : ℵ₀ ≤ a → ℵ₀ ≤ b → a * b = max a b` (built on `mul_eq_self`) computes infinite products.

annotations.label: Infinite product equals sum

anchor.snippet: the product of two infinite cardinal numbers is equal to their sum

annotations.note: `mul_eq_max` and `add_eq_max` both equal `max`, so a*b = a+b for infinite cardinals, but no single decl states the equality directly.

annotations.label: Cardinal division

anchor.snippet: there exists a cardinal κ such that μ · κ = π if and only if μ ≤ π

annotations.note: `dvd_of_le_of_aleph0_le` (μ ≠ 0, μ ≤ π, ℵ₀ ≤ π → μ ∣ π) with `le_of_dvd` gives the iff for infinite π.

annotations.label: Cardinal exponentiation via function space

anchor.snippet: the set of all functions from Y to X

annotations.note: `power_def : #α ^ #β = #(β → α)` defines exponentiation as the function space.

annotations.label: κ^0 = 1 (empty function)

anchor.snippet: see empty function

annotations.note: `power_zero : a ^ 0 = 1`.

annotations.label: 0^μ = 0 for μ ≥ 1

anchor.snippet: If μ ≥ 1, then 0 μ = 0

annotations.note: `zero_power : a ≠ 0 → (0:Cardinal) ^ a = 0`.

annotations.label: 1^μ = 1
annotations.note: `one_power : (1:Cardinal) ^ a = 1`.

annotations.label: κ^1 = κ

anchor.snippet: κ 1 = κ

annotations.note: `power_one : a ^ 1 = a`.

annotations.label: Exponent addition law

anchor.snippet: κ μ + ν = κ μ · κ ν

annotations.note: `power_add : a ^ (b + c) = a ^ b * a ^ c`.

annotations.label: Exponent multiplication law

anchor.snippet: κ μ · ν = ( κ μ ) ν

annotations.note: `power_mul : a ^ (b * c) = (a ^ b) ^ c`.

annotations.label: Product raised to a power

anchor.snippet: ( κ · μ ) ν = κ ν · μ ν

annotations.note: `mul_power : (a * b) ^ c = a ^ c * b ^ c`.

annotations.label: Exponentiation is non-decreasing

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation is non-decreasing in both arguments

annotations.note: `power_le_power_right` and `power_le_power_left` give monotonicity in base and exponent.

annotations.label: Cantor: 2^|X| > |X|

anchor.snippet: is the cardinality of the power set of the set X

annotations.note: `cantor : a < 2 ^ a`, with `mk_set : #(Set α) = 2 ^ #α`.

annotations.label: No largest cardinal

anchor.snippet: This proves that no largest cardinal exists

annotations.note: The `NoMaxOrder Cardinal` instance (via `cantor`) shows every cardinal has a strictly greater one.

annotations.label: Cardinals form a proper class

anchor.snippet: the class of cardinals is a proper class

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Cardinal.{u}` is a type in universe u+1, not a proper class; the closest fact is `mk_cardinal : #Cardinal = univ`.

annotations.label: Finite bases, infinite exponent equal

anchor.snippet: If κ and μ are both finite and greater than 1, and ν is infinite

annotations.note: `nat_power_eq : ℵ₀ ≤ c → 2 ≤ n → (n:Cardinal) ^ c = 2 ^ c` shows all finite bases ≥ 2 give the same value.

annotations.label: Infinite base, finite exponent

anchor.snippet: If κ is infinite and μ is finite and non-zero

annotations.note: `power_nat_eq : ℵ₀ ≤ c → 1 ≤ n → c ^ n = c`.

annotations.label: Bounds on κ^μ

anchor.snippet: at least one of them is infinite

annotations.note: Related bounds like `power_self_eq` and `power_eq_two_power` exist, but the exact max(κ,2^μ) ≤ κ^μ ≤ 2^max(κ,μ) double bound is not stated.

annotations.label: Kőnig's theorem inequalities

anchor.snippet: Using Kőnig's theorem , one can prove

annotations.note: `lt_power_cof_ord : ℵ₀ ≤ c → c < c ^ c.ord.cof` and `lt_cof_ord_power` are the König consequences (base `sum_lt_prod`).

annotations.label: Cardinal roots

anchor.snippet: given an infinite cardinal κ and a finite cardinal μ greater than 0

annotations.note: `power_nat_eq : ℵ₀ ≤ κ → 1 ≤ n → κ ^ n = κ` shows the n-th root of an infinite κ is κ.

annotations.label: Existence of cardinal logarithm

anchor.snippet: there may or may not be a cardinal λ satisfying

annotations.note: There is no cardinal logarithm in Mathlib (only `Ordinal.log` for ordinals).

annotations.label: Logarithm of an infinite cardinal

anchor.snippet: The logarithm of an infinite cardinal number κ is defined as the least cardinal number μ

annotations.note: No `Cardinal.log` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuum hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The continuum hypothesis (CH) states that there are no cardinals strictly between

annotations.note: CH (𝔠 = ℵ₁) is not defined as a proposition; only `aleph_one_le_continuum : ℵ₁ ≤ 𝔠` is available.

annotations.label: Generalized continuum hypothesis

anchor.snippet: the generalized continuum hypothesis (GCH) states that for every infinite cardinal

annotations.note: GCH is only mentioned in a docstring; it is not formalized as a definition.

annotations.label: CH and GCH independent of ZFC

anchor.snippet: have been proved to be independent of the usual axioms of set theory

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize forcing or independence results about CH/GCH.

annotations.label: Easton's theorem

anchor.snippet: Easton's theorem shows that, for regular cardinals

annotations.note: Easton's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite sets: bijection iff equal count

anchor.snippet: there is a bijection between two finite sets if and only if they have the same number of elements

annotations.note: `Fintype.card_eq : card α = card β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` for finite types.

annotations.label: Denumerable (countably infinite) sets

anchor.snippet: he called all sets having a bijection with N denumerable

annotations.note: `Denumerable α` is the class of types with an explicit bijection to ℕ.

annotations.label: Transfinite cardinal numbers

anchor.snippet: He called the cardinal numbers of infinite sets transfinite cardinal numbers

annotations.note: Infinite cardinals are exactly those with `ℵ₀ ≤ #α` (`infinite_iff`), but no dedicated 'transfinite cardinal' definition exists.

annotations.label: Unbounded subset of N equinumerous with N

anchor.snippet: any unbounded subset of N has the same cardinality as N

annotations.note: An infinite (unbounded) subset of ℕ is countable and infinite, hence denumerable (cardinality ℵ₀ = #ℕ).

annotations.label: Ordered pairs of naturals are denumerable

anchor.snippet: the set of all ordered pairs of natural numbers is denumerable

annotations.note: `Denumerable.prod` gives `Denumerable (ℕ × ℕ)`; also `aleph0_mul_aleph0 : ℵ₀ * ℵ₀ = ℵ₀`.

annotations.label: Real algebraic numbers are denumerable

anchor.snippet: the set of all real algebraic numbers is also denumerable

annotations.note: `Algebraic.countable : Set.Countable {x | IsAlgebraic R x}` gives countability of algebraic numbers over any countable base field.

annotations.label: Reals exceed naturals in cardinality

anchor.snippet: the set of real numbers has cardinality greater than that of N

annotations.note: `mk_real : #ℝ = 𝔠` with `aleph0_lt_continuum` (and `not_countable_real`) shows ℝ exceeds ℕ.

annotations.label: Smallest transfinite cardinal and successors

anchor.snippet: there is a smallest transfinite cardinal number

annotations.note: ℵ₀ is the least infinite cardinal (`aleph0_le_mk`), with successors via `aleph`/`Order.succ` (e.g. `succ_aleph0 = ℵ₁`).

annotations.label: Gödel and Cohen: independence of CH

anchor.snippet: Kurt Gödel showed that the continuum hypothesis cannot be disproved from ZFC

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the Gödel/Cohen consistency and independence results for CH.
